_source: 依方向 + 關係類型 + gap 類型，給出不同的對治建議
_gap_types: drain=我方向消耗大, their_side=對方獲益多, my_side=我獲益多

衰.spouse: You're constantly pouring energy into your partner in this marriage. Talk to them regularly about what you need — don't just push through in silence
衰.lover: You're giving more in this relationship. Watch your energy levels, and speak up when you're running low
衰.friend: You're giving more in this friendship. If it feels off, adjust how often you meet — you don't have to force it
衰.colleague: You're putting in more than your counterpart. Make sure your contributions are visible — don't carry things quietly
衰.family: You're the backbone of this relationship. If you keep giving long-term, remember to take care of yourself too
衰.parent: You keep giving to them. This is driven by the energy structure — pay attention to your own state
衰.ex: Looking back, you were being drained the whole time. Were you always receiving care but losing yourself in the process? This insight is for your next relationship

安.spouse: You're the stabilizing foundation of your family. Share the load with your partner — don't carry all the chores and decisions alone
安.lover: You've always been the one creating a stable environment. Let your partner do something for you too
安.friend: You're always the reliable one. Asking for help once in a while isn't a weakness
安.colleague: You build the infrastructure while they enjoy the results. Make sure your work shows up in performance reviews
安.family: You keep the household running. Say something when you're tired — nobody knows unless you tell them
安.parent: You're the builder of this relationship. Giving is in your nature, but you need someone to support you too
安.ex: You spent all your time building a stable environment for them. Did you feel lighter after the breakup? If so, you were being hollowed out without realizing it

危.spouse: The early friction in this marriage fell mostly on you. It gets better with time — give each other space
危.lover: You're absorbing most of the pressure in this relationship. It stabilizes after the rough patch, but take care of yourself along the way
危.friend: You're under more pressure in this friendship. Group settings feel easier than one-on-one
危.colleague: You're carrying more pressure at work. Social settings build trust more easily than meeting rooms
危.family: The friction pressure lands on your side. It gets smoother with age — give it time
危.parent: You carry more pressure in this relationship. Formal occasions feel more natural than everyday interactions
危.ex: You absorbed most of the friction in this relationship. If leaving felt like relief, that pressure wasn't growth — it was drain

親.spouse: You naturally nurture your partner — that's good energy in a marriage. But remember to be taken care of too
親.lover: You're the giver, constantly creating stability for them. Make sure your own needs are being met
親.friend: You're the one running this friendship. Giving is auspicious, but if you're always the one initiating, pay attention
親.colleague: You're mentoring them at work. Make sure this mentoring relationship is recognized by management
親.family: You're the glue holding the family together. Your dedication keeps things solid, but giving isn't an obligation
親.parent: You keep nurturing them. That's your role, but you deserve something back too
親.ex: You were always nurturing them. After parting, ask yourself: did they become better because of you? Did you lose yourself in the giving?

友.spouse: Your social contributions in this marriage aren't being reciprocated equally. You built the network, they use it, but your needs get ignored
友.lover: You've been actively building this relationship, but their responses keep fading. Your effort is being consumed
友.friend: You've always been the proactive one, but their responses keep getting more half-hearted. Your social energy is going to waste
友.colleague: You lead cross-department coordination, but others take the credit. Your effort is being consumed
友.family: You're the one everyone calls to handle things, but nobody ever asks what you need. You're doing thankless work
友.parent: Your investment in this parent keeps draining away. It's not that you're not good enough — they're just not receiving
友.ex: You kept reaching out but they never responded. After parting, you realized your effort was completely one-sided

栄.spouse: Your partner has been lifting you up more than you realize. Their support style may not match your expectations
栄.lover: They invest more in your growth than you've noticed. It's not that you're ungrateful — you may just be overlooking it
栄.friend: They've been pushing you forward more than you think. Recall when they gave you a boost
栄.colleague: Their contribution to your growth is bigger than you've noticed. Don't underestimate their role
栄.family: They've been quietly supporting you, but you might be taking it for granted
栄.parent: This parent has pushed your growth more than you realize. Much of their support only becomes visible later
栄.ex: They elevated you more than you felt at the time. Looking back, many of your improvements came from that relationship

友.spouse: Your partner enjoys more of the social benefits of this marriage. The network you built serves them too
友.lover: You're the giver; they receive more. Check periodically whether your effort is being valued
友.friend: The connections and trust you've built — they're using them too. Make sure they don't only show up when they need help
友.colleague: Your cross-department network is being leveraged by them. Make sure the collaboration is two-way
友.family: You care about them more actively. That's who you are, but their response matters too
友.parent: You give actively; they receive. That's the natural flow of energy
友.ex: You were the giver in this relationship. If they vanished completely after the split, they wanted your resources — not you

成.spouse: Your partner has grown more in this marriage. You drove their achievements — don't forget your own goals
成.lover: They've gained a sense of achievement from you. Make sure you're growing too
成.friend: They've learned more from being with you. But the shared learning process itself has value
成.colleague: They've grown more from the collaboration. Your driving force is the key — make sure it's recognized
成.family: They've grown more with your help. Your role is important — don't underestimate your contribution
成.parent: They've benefited from your guidance. That's the natural structure of the nurturing direction
成.ex: They grew more by your side. After parting, ask yourself: were your results taken from you? Is what you learned still with you?

壊.spouse: Your partner gives you more stability than you've absorbed. The sutra says 'the rest is not bearable' — daily life won't be smooth, but the direction is right
壊.lover: They give you more stability than you feel. It's hard to reciprocate equally — just be consciously grateful
壊.friend: They've supported you in this friendship more than you realize. The imbalance is structural, not your fault
壊.colleague: Their foundational support in this partnership is bigger than you've noticed
壊.family: Their way of caring may not be visible to you. The imbalance is natural — growing is the best way to give back
壊.parent: This parent gave you more stability than you think. Accept the structural imbalance and give back where you can
壊.ex: They gave you more stability than you felt at the time. The things you're rebuilding now were actually built by them

命.spouse: You two are very alike, but they've benefited slightly more from this marriage. No need to change anything deliberately
命.lover: In this same-star relationship, they benefit slightly more. Don't keep score — focus on growing together
命.friend: They've gained more from mirroring you. That's normal — no adjustments needed
命.colleague: You think alike but they benefit more. Make sure your contributions are visible
命.family: Same type but they benefit slightly more. Don't keep score — focus on moving forward together
命.parent: In this same-star structure, they benefit slightly more. Natural phenomenon — no forced adjustments needed
命.ex: You were too alike, and only after parting could you see each other's issues clearly. Looking back now, the things you disliked in them — aren't those things you have too?

業.spouse: They've gained more from this marriage, but your accumulation period will pay off. Give it time
業.lover: They benefit more. It's not that you're not good enough — the karma-side rhythm is just slower
業.friend: They benefit from your patience. Long-term, this connection will repay you in unexpected ways
業.colleague: Their growth is more visible. You're in your accumulation phase — your investment will pay off later
業.family: They've gained more from you. The weight of karmic ties takes time to resolve
業.parent: They benefit more. The karma-side rhythm is slow — doesn't mean it's valueless
業.ex: After parting, you realized this relationship never really progressed. It's not your fault — the karma-side rhythm just stalls

胎.spouse: They've grown faster in this marriage. You're the potential type — you'll break through when the time is right
胎.lover: They benefit more. The embryo-side is still brewing — your value will show up in the future
胎.friend: They've gained more from this friendship. Your growth is latent — it needs time
胎.colleague: They're growing faster. You're accumulating experience — it'll come in handy later
胎.family: They benefit more. You're on the receiving end of nurturing — the best repayment is your own growth
胎.parent: They benefit more. The embryo-side's value is long-term
胎.ex: Someday after parting, you might suddenly understand what this relationship meant. The embryo-side's value is long-term — no rush

親.spouse: Your nurturing helped your partner grow substantially. You're the driving force in this marriage — make sure your own needs are met too
親.lover: You nurtured them in this relationship, and they became better because of you. But are you being taken care of?
親.friend: The friend you've nurtured has grown because of your help. Make sure your effort is being appreciated
親.colleague: The colleague you mentored has grown through your investment. Make sure your mentoring contribution is visible to management
親.family: Your dedication in the family helped them grow. Your sacrifice has value, but don't sacrifice until you forget yourself
親.parent: Your care for this parent gives them great peace of mind. This is the right direction — but take care of yourself too
親.ex: The person you nurtured became better because of you. After parting, ask yourself: what did this giving mean for you?

安.spouse: The home environment you built let your partner grow in stability. Your building has value — but don't focus so much on creating the setting that you forget yourself
安.lover: Your partner really enjoys the stable environment you built for this relationship. Make sure they're also maintaining it, not just enjoying it
安.friend: The stable environment you provide gives your friend great comfort. But being reliable shouldn't become being taken for granted
安.colleague: They enjoy the results of the systems and processes you built. Make sure your infrastructure work counts in your reviews
安.family: Your effort to maintain family stability benefits everyone. But stability isn't one person's responsibility
安.parent: The secure environment you built for your parent puts them at ease. Good direction — but taking care of yourself is what makes it sustainable
安.ex: They enjoyed the stable environment you built. After parting, ask yourself: did you take care of yourself while you were building?

衰.spouse: Your partner cares for you more than you might realize. Their way of caring may not match what you expect
衰.lover: They give you more than you feel. It's not that they're lacking — you might not be noticing
衰.friend: They support you more than you're aware of. Think about what they've quietly done for you
衰.colleague: They contribute more to the partnership than you've noticed. Don't overlook their support
衰.family: Their care may not be obvious to you. Action-based love is harder to notice
衰.parent: This parent gave you more than you think. Some care only becomes visible when you grow older
衰.ex: They cared for you more than you felt at the time. Looking back after the breakup, you may notice things you missed

栄.spouse: You're the beneficiary in this marriage — your partner has been quietly lifting you up. Remember to thank them and give back
栄.lover: You've gained more from this relationship. Do something for them proactively — don't take it for granted
栄.friend: You've gained more from this friendship. Make an effort to do something for them once in a while
栄.colleague: You've benefited more from the collaboration. Actively help highlight their contributions
栄.family: You're the one being cared for in the family. Don't take it for granted — express your gratitude
栄.parent: You benefit from their effort. Remember to give back
栄.ex: You were the beneficiary. After parting, ask yourself: did you ever properly thank them? This regret can be made up in your next relationship

衰.spouse: You're very well taken care of in this marriage. Enjoy it but don't lose your independence
衰.lover: They take great care of you. Accept it while keeping your own center of gravity
衰.friend: They actively care for you more than you do for them. Enjoy it but stay independent
衰.colleague: Their support in this partnership is spot-on. Remember to reciprocate — don't take it for granted
衰.family: You're getting plenty of care. Accept it but don't lose your ability to handle things yourself
衰.parent: This parent takes excellent care of you. Maintain your independence — don't rely on them for everything
衰.ex: They took great care of you back then. If you felt lost after the breakup, you were too dependent. Keep that in mind for next time

友.spouse: Your partner builds networks for you in this marriage. Reciprocate their social investment
友.lover: They give more actively than you. Their effort deserves gratitude
友.friend: They actively maintain this friendship. You receive more — initiate plans once in a while too
友.colleague: They build connections for you. Make sure you're reciprocating their investment
友.family: They care about you more actively. Remember to care about them too
友.parent: They give more actively. Respond to their goodwill
友.ex: They actively maintained this relationship. If you only realized after parting that you never initiated, remember to reach out first in your next relationship

安.spouse: Your partner is the stable foundation of the family, and you enjoy the environment they've built. Express your thanks
安.lover: They've been creating a stable environment for you. Don't treat their effort as background noise
安.friend: They're your most reliable friend. Their effort deserves to be seen
安.colleague: They handle the infrastructure while you enjoy the stable environment. Acknowledge their contribution
安.family: They keep the household running. Their effort is invisible but indispensable
安.parent: They build a stable environment for you. Express your thanks
安.ex: They built a stable environment for you. Can you hold up your life on your own after the split? That's the real test of independence

壊.spouse: You're receiving stability. The classic warning reminds you it's hard to repay in other ways — be consciously grateful
壊.lover: You benefit from their effort. The imbalance is structural — the best repayment is your own growth
壊.friend: You're the beneficiary of this friendship. Their effort keeps you grounded — don't forget to be grateful
壊.colleague: You benefit from the infrastructure they built. Use the resources well, but know where they came from
壊.family: You're the one being cared for. The imbalance is natural — growth is the best repayment
壊.parent: You benefit from their effort. The way to repay is to become a better version of yourself
壊.ex: You were on the receiving end. Only after parting did you realize how much they gave. Gratitude can be late — but it shouldn't be absent

命.spouse: You benefit slightly more in this marriage. Same-star pairing — don't keep score, focus on growing together
命.lover: You benefit slightly more. No need to adjust — focus on moving forward together
命.friend: You've gained more from this mirroring relationship. Enjoy the understanding but also give to them
命.colleague: You benefit more. Make sure you're reciprocating their investment
命.family: You benefit slightly more. Don't keep score — growing together is what matters most
命.parent: You benefit more. Natural phenomenon — thank them
命.ex: You two were very alike, and you grew faster after parting. It's not that they were bad — people too similar just tend to stagnate together

業.spouse: You've gained more from this marriage than your partner. They're in their accumulation phase — give them time and space
業.lover: You benefit more. Their slower pace doesn't mean they don't care
業.friend: You've gained more from this friendship. Their way of reciprocating might not match what you expect
業.colleague: You've benefited more from the collaboration. They need more time before results show
業.family: You benefit more. They carry the weight of karmic ties — give them space
業.parent: You benefit more. Their value takes time to become visible
業.ex: You benefited more, though it wasn't obvious. Looking back, the patience and waiting this relationship taught you — that's your biggest takeaway

胎.spouse: You've grown faster in this marriage. Your partner is the potential type — their breakthrough comes later
胎.lover: You've grown faster. The embryo-side's value is long-term — don't rush it
胎.friend: You've grown more from this friendship. Their return will show up in the future
胎.colleague: You've grown faster. They're brewing — a breakthrough will come
胎.family: You've grown more. They need time — the best support is patience
胎.parent: You've grown faster. They're the potential type — give it time
胎.ex: You grew faster. Parting doesn't mean failure — sometimes it's just that your rhythms were out of sync

成.spouse: You've grown more in this marriage. Your partner drove your achievements — share your progress with them
成.lover: You've gained more of a sense of achievement from them. Remember to drive their growth too
成.friend: You've gained more from learning together. Invite them to tackle more challenging things with you
成.colleague: You've grown more from the collaboration. Help them find growth opportunities too
成.family: You've grown more with their help. Share your results
成.parent: You've grown more. The way to give back is to share your progress
成.ex: You gained achievement from them. The growth you took with you is permanent — be grateful and move forward

危.spouse: You benefit slightly more in this marriage. Your partner absorbs more of the friction pressure — be considerate
危.lover: You benefit more. They bear more of the relationship pressure — take care of their feelings
危.friend: You benefit more from this friendship. They absorb more social pressure
危.colleague: You benefit more from the collaboration. They're under pressure — support them
危.family: You benefit more. They absorb the friction pressure — give them time
危.parent: You benefit more. They carry more pressure — be considerate
危.ex: They bore more pressure than you. If you're still in touch, a belated thank-you wouldn't be too much
